audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Thessalonica, Berea, Athens, Areopagus, unknown god, divine nearness, poets, resurrection response.

verses.restored: Passing through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Judeans.
According to Paul's custom, he went in to them, and on three Sabbaths reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
opening and setting before them that it was necessary for the Messiah to suffer and rise from the dead, and that this Jesus whom I announce to you is the Messiah.
Some of them were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, also a great multitude of reverent Greeks and not a few leading women.

But the Judeans, becoming jealous and taking some wicked men from the marketplace rabble, gathering a crowd, threw the city into uproar. Standing over Jason's house, they were seeking to bring them out to the people.
Not finding them, they dragged Jason and some siblings before the city rulers, crying, These ones who have turned the inhabited world upside down have come here also,
whom Jason has received; and all these act against Caesar's decrees, saying there is another king, Jesus.
They troubled the crowd and the city rulers hearing these things.
After taking security from Jason and the rest, they released them.
The siblings immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea. Arriving, they went into the synagogue of the Judeans.
These were more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica, who received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily whether these things were so.
Therefore many of them trusted, and not a few of the honorable Greek women and men.
But when the Judeans from Thessalonica knew that the word of the Divine was also announced by Paul in Berea, they came there also, shaking and troubling the crowds.
Then immediately the siblings sent Paul away to go as far as the sea, but Silas and Timothy remained there.
Those conducting Paul brought him as far as Athens, and receiving a command for Silas and Timothy to come to him as quickly as possible, they departed.
While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he observed the city full of idols.
So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Judeans and reverent ones, and in the marketplace every day with those who happened to be there.

Some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also conversed with him. Some said, What does this seed-picker want to say? Others said, He seems to be an announcer of foreign divinities, because he was announcing good news of Jesus and the resurrection.
Taking hold of him, they brought him to the Areopagus, saying, Are we able to know what this new teaching is that is spoken by you?
For you are bringing certain strange things to our ears; therefore we wish to know what these things mean.
Now all Athenians and the visiting foreigners spent their time in nothing other than saying or hearing something newer.
Paul, standing in the midst of the Areopagus, said, Men of Athens, I behold that in every way you are very reverent toward divine things.
For passing through and observing the objects of your worship, I found also an altar on which had been written, To an unknown god. What therefore you revere without knowing, this I announce to you.
The Divine who made the world and all things in it, being Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in handmade temples,
nor is served by human hands as though needing anything, since he himself gives to all life and breath and all things.
From one he made every nation of humans to dwell upon all the face of the earth, appointing ordered times and boundaries of their dwelling,
so that they might seek the Divine, if indeed they might feel after him and find him, though he is not far from each one of us.
For in him we live and move and are, as also some of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring.
Being therefore offspring of the Divine, we ought not to think the Divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an engraving of human craft and imagination.
Therefore, overlooking the times of ignorance, the Divine now commands all humans everywhere to turn/realign,
because he has set a day in which he is about to judge the inhabited world in righteousness/justice by a man whom he appointed, giving trust to all by raising him from the dead.
Hearing resurrection of the dead, some mocked, but others said, We will hear you again about this.
Thus Paul went out from their midst.
But some men joined him and trusted, among whom also were Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.
